I could not see paying 100 dollars for an off the shelf part, that if it had problems I could not fix. I have two buck converters per old monitor and could rebuild the mechanical regulator.  If the supply of cheap parts from china goes away it will be thinking like this that could saves your life .

author: @morski_kapetan
text:
I have 2 critical requirements for my future charge controller:
1. It can handle any (really any) voltage. 
2. It never breaks.

replies.text: - not sure in a real world you will get number 2 mate - because of that you will need to build in a contingency for when it does break - so 'breaking' will be invisible to you - basically - everything breaks if want unbreakable the strategy is to build in a coping mechanism for when it breaks

author: @morski_kapetan
text: Thanks! This is a great video, really smart! I needed this and will always need lessons of this type. I also think that people who know much more about electricity than I do should also be reminded of the basics, of the elementary concepts. It is essential for any level of knowledge to always go back to basics...we tend to get lazy and think that we already 'got it'. We are looking for ready-made solutions, like for sugar candy. The only good solution is the one I come up with on my own, using any advice and guidance I find appropriate.

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
From about 3 minutes in I get the sense that you are headed towards talking about a transistor. That magnetic coil in your solenoid switch could in very basic terms be likened to the gate (or base) of the transistor.
I use a solar charge controller only because it was cheap alternative, I paid about $25 shipped for a 60 amp solar charge controller. Probably because the positive and negative solar connections were mis labeled and I had to swap the leads to get it working. Anyway I have all the house lights working on solar powered 12v LED's and 235 Ah AGM batteries.
Love the way you simplify everything and make it understandable to s point you go back and see options you should have seen before.

replies.author: @stuffoflardohfortheloveof
replies.text: You’ve hit on something that I often think about. I started coding in my teens some 40yrs ago and, although I haven’t kept up with newer languages/concepts, I do struggle with accepting that a machine can think for itself when all I’ve ever known is that every part of a machine’s actions are based on some subroutine path hidden somewhere in the programming. I’m certainly open to being educated differently but surely ‘deep blue’ (who beat Kasparov all those years ago) was little more than a huge database of chess moves, ie didn’t really think for itself? To me, the concept of a self-driving car is nothing more than a massively improved version of the chess computer yet is it being portrayed as ‘thinking’ for itself? Here’s something else to philosophise on... is the human brain the result of a massive program?........I’ll leave it there......😁

replies.author: @BiosWars
replies.text: @stuffoflardohfortheloveof Really great to know that I'm not the only Computer Engineer that thinks " Calculates" along the same lines.

replies.text: Neural nets are not  programmed,  They learn from experience.. like you do

replies.author: @BiosWars
replies.text: @beascene6998 A neural network is a series of algorithms that endeavors to recognize underlying relationships in a set of data through a process that mimics the way the human brain operates. In this sense, neural networks refer to systems of neurons, either organic or artificial in nature, they are still programmed by humans. Hence, the noted word algorithms.

replies.author: @stuffoflardohfortheloveof
replies.text: @beascene6998 See, this is where is gets grey for me. I may be unable to grasp the concept and, believe me, I do try to see other perspectives but (and I accept I may be completely wrong) please try to see it from this perspective for a mo......is not machine learning simply another way of saying that the ‘machine’ is only following a series of ‘if...then...’ type of subroutines, albeit massive subroutines? In other words it’s not thinking for itself at all. Mind you, this is why I mentioned the human brain earlier as I do ponder on the thought that we may all be an advanced system of subroutines that appears to portray the image of self awareness and learning...Oops, ill stop now as it’s getting deep......good conversation though.....👍😀

author: @beascene6998
text:
The NEED in most solar charge controller supplications is the harvest as much solar energy as possible. Solar panels are 18-20% efficient in the very best of cases to begin with. A PWM controller will apply some  charging voltage to your battery for a short period of time in an on/off type of arrangement similar to your electromechanical voltage regulator. The sun is sending energy continuously however.

What happens to the energy not absorbed by the battery when the controller is switched off? 

Answer: waisted... unless it is sent to a ‘dump load’. This is usually a water heater element. Converting solar energy to electricity and then to heat is TERRIBLY inefficient, but so is wasting up to half or more of the solar panel output.

author: @MerwinARTist
text: Great discussion!  One of the questions I have had regarding controllers .. is whether or not the controller is aware of the kind of battery (lead acid, lithium, etc) or not.  I guess it doesn't matter since a switch will cut off power to any light .. without regard to it's being an LED or Incandescent.  Some of the information I had been looking at recommended the MPPT controller because it would pack more energy into a battery on a cloudy day .. for those living in a van and dependent upon solar to get through a few days of rainy weather .. those individuals are concerned about the long term endurance of the batteries so don't want to overheat them etc. but still keep the charge level high so the battery doesn't fall under a 50% energy level.  Thanks for discussing this!  :-)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the controller is really going to be aware of nothing mate - it will depend on how you set it up - a pre-bought controller may or may not be adaptable to a specific battery depending on the way the manufacturer made it - basically are there switches in it you are allowed to press that can configure it to be able to use the kind of battery you have

replies.author: @MerwinARTist
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Thank you for the reply!  Interesting!

author: @johnwilson3668
text:
Sometimes you need MPPT controllers like in my own situation . My solar panel bank consists  of 30v panels charging a large bank of 12v caravan batteries which supply a 3000w 
full sine wave inverter. The MPPT controller has a battery voltage sensor which sets the output to 12v . In the process of converting the voltage from 30v to 12v ,the excess energy is converted 
into an increased amperage output .The charge never goes less than 13.5v even on a dull day, as it is intelligently stepped up or down depending on conditions to suit the voltage of the battery. I had no option to use any other controller than a MPPT with having 30v panels.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sometimes yes - sometimes no

author: @NorthernKitty
text: What I always wondered about MPPT controllers is, how do they actually regulate charging?  The MPPT acronym really only refers to the job of tracking optimal charge conditions ("Maximum Power Point Tracking").  Is it really just a "smart" PWM charge controller, then, or does it actually regulate the voltage via another manner?  I think sometimes the labels/terms we use clouds things further, distancing us from the most basic functions and turning things into the "mystery" you spoke of.

replies.author: @01Alexsimps
replies.text: I think mppt involves some buck boost circuitry to bump up the voltage to something useful even when somewhat cloudy

replies.author: @vylbird8014
replies.text: It's a buck converter (Or in some models, buck-boost) with the switching frequency and on-time set by a microcontroller that adjusts it continually to maximise charge current, until it determines the battery is full.

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: @vylbird8014 Thanks!!  I've looked at a lot of MPPT controllers and they never mention how the voltage is regulated, so I always wondered if they were simply a "smart" PWM. I would think it could be important to know about that difference for certain applications.  Thanks also to Alternative Space Misc and 01Alexsimps for your posts, they were equally helpful!  😊

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes I think so too

author: @ancapftw9113
text: MPPT produces the most power, especially on cloudy days, but it isn't always nessecery.

replies.author: @vylbird8014
replies.text: Depends on your setup. If you're only running a very small scale system, like powering lighting in an out-building or some remote sensors, it can easily work out cheaper just to an an extra panel. Fifty watts or panel and a dirt-cheap PWM controller still generate more than twenty-five watts of panel and an expensive MPPT. Once you're up to a few hundred watts though, the benefits of MPPT become clear.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: for sure mate

author: @johnoddvar1967
text: I did it my ovn way when i made a solar system for a cabin. I got a used car battery from a gas station near by and i hadd a smal solar panel. I regulatid it with a bunsh of silicon diodes soldered in series i think its about twenty. it ceeps the chargeing about to 14,2v Utilising the ca 0,5v over a silicon diode. its fitet in a garden hose betveen the poles of the battery. It has funccioned for about 8 years now whitout problems. Then i have been looking at other charge controllers and they al cut the curent. I perfectly agree that its several ways to do things. It does a few led lights and a sigaret lighter so he can charge his phone.
